coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid with a water-dispersible terbium-metal organic framework
coredata.dc:creator: Bhardwaj, Neha
Bhardwaj, Sanjeev
Mehta, Jyotsana
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Deep, Akash
coredata.dc:description: The sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid (DPA) is strongly associated with the sensing of bacterial organisms in food and many types of environmental samples. To date, the demand for a sensitive detection method for bacterial toxicity has increased remarkably. Herein, we investigated the DPA detection potential of a water-dispersible terbium-metal organic framework (Tb-MOF) based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The Tb-MOF showed a highly sensitive ability to detect DPA at a limit of detection of 0.04nM (linear range of detection: 1nM to 5µM) and also offered enhanced selectivity from other commonly associated organic molecules. The present study provides a basis for the application of Tb-MOF for direct, convenient, highly sensitive, and specific detection of DPA in the actual samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal organic framework
Sensor
Fluorescence
Terbium
Dipicolinic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Absorption and emission characteristics of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6
Studies of photoluminescence detection of endospores

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture, Science, & Technology Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge that financial assistance was provided by CSIR-India (Grant no. OMEGA/PSC0202/2.2.5 ). The authors thank the Director of CSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh. KHK also acknowledges support from a grant of the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), which is funded by the Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology ( MEST ) (No. 2006–0093848 ) and by the Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture, Science, & Technology Development (Project title: Study on model development to control odor from hogpens, Project No. PJ01052101 ), which is funded by the Rural Development Administration , Republic of Korea.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Coordination modes of Tb atoms with carboxylate groups of the 1,3,5-BTC ligand.

(a) XRD spectra of synthesized Tb(BTC)(H2O)6; (b) Confocal laser scanning microscopic image showing the fluorescent nature of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6; (c) Optically magnified image of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6; and (d) FTIR spectrum of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6, inset showing the magnified spectrum between 1200 and 1800cm−1 (T refers to transmission).
(a) Absorbance spectrum and (b) photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6 when mixed with different concentrations of DPA; and (b) Range of linear responses of the sensory material depicting changes in PL intensity as a function of DPA concentration (1nM to 5 μM).
Specificity in the quenching of PL intensity of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6 toward DPA (5μM) only. The concentrations of p-phenylene diacetic acid (PDA) and isonicotinic acid (INA) were 10μM.
Performance comparison between various types of fluorescent probes employed for the detection of DPA.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid with a water-dispersible terbium-metal organic framework

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Scientific Instruments Organisation (CSIR-CSIO), Sector 30C, Chandigarh, 160030 India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, CSIR-CSIO, Sector 30C, Chandigarh, 160030 India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, CSIR-CSIO

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-Ro, Seoul, 04763 Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid (DPA) is strongly associated with the sensing of bacterial organisms in food and many types of environmental samples. To date, the demand for a sensitive detection method for bacterial toxicity has increased remarkably. Herein, we investigated the DPA detection potential of a water-dispersible terbium-metal organic framework (Tb-MOF) based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The Tb-MOF showed a highly sensitive ability to detect DPA at a limit of detection of 0.04nM (linear range of detection: 1nM to 5µM) and also offered enhanced selectivity from other commonly associated organic molecules. The present study provides a basis for the application of Tb-MOF for direct, convenient, highly sensitive, and specific detection of DPA in the actual samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The formation of spore (toxin) by bacterial organisms is a significant concern worldwide. Several bacterial species (e.g., Bacillus cereus, Bacillus subtilis, Clostridium botulinum, Clostridium perfringens, Clostridium difficile, and Bacillus anthracis) produce spores in their dormant states. These spores are highly resistant to heat, biocides, and radiation treatments and can cause infectious diseases (e.g., anthrax, tetanus, food poisoning, and diarrhea) in animals and humans. Clostridium perfringens (C. perfringes) is a Gram-positive obligate anaerobic spore-forming bacteria responsible for food poisoning and diarrhea in humans. About one million cases of foodborne illness are estimated to be caused by C. perfringens each year in the United States (y Center for Disease Control and Prevention, 2016). C. perfringens spores are metabolically dormant multilayered structures that can persist in the environment for long periods to cause recurrence of several related diseases (Raju et al., 2007). Infections by Clostridium sp. are the result of bacterial spore ingestion by a susceptible host. The bacterial spores then transfer to the gastrointestinal (GI) tract and germinate, resulting in release of dipicolinic acid (DPA, pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid). The detection of DPA can directly help in the quantification of the related bacterial spores as it constitutes about 5 – 15% of the total dry weight of the spores in the sample (Gould and Hurst, 1969). The analysis of DPA as a biomarker, has been practiced by technical experts in the diagnostic industry as a means to determine the number of bacteria in feces (Paredes-Sabja et al., 2009). The detection of DPA has also been a favorable option to assess endospores in sediments (Fichtel et al., 2007). The monitoring of DPA in food samples has been employed to optimize the conditions required for the heat treatment of microbial spores (Kort et al., 2005). Thus, the quantitative analysis of DPA is of great practical significance as an indicator of micro-organism contamination as well as monitoring of effectiveness of several food sterilization procedures (Yang and Ponce, 2011).

Various biological and chemical methods have been developed for the detection of bacterial spores. Biological methods include polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and immunoassays (Bode et al., 2004; King et al., 2003), while chemical methods include surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Bell et al., 2005), high-pressure liquid chromatography (Paulus, 1981), electrochemical detection (Tan et al., 2011), and fluorescent detection (Lee et al., 2013). Fluorescent sensing of DPA with lanthanide ions has been recognized as a promising method for the detection of bacterial endospores. Lanthanide ion-DPA complexes offer unique optical signals to facilitate DPA detection (Li and Selvin, 1995). Terbium ion-based luminescence assays have been very successful for quantitative detection of DPA due to selective energy transfer of DPA to the terbium ions (Curiel et al., 2012; Hindle and Hall, 1999).

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have recently been explored as potential tool for the detection of DPA molecules (Rieter et al., 2007; Xu et al., 2012b). The MOFs are porous crystalline materials possessing excellent properties such as high surface area, tunable porosity, and optical and electronic properties. MOFs have been used for a variety of applications such as the storage and separation of small molecules (Murray et al., 2009; Yang et al., 2009), molecular sieving (Majumder et al., 2012), sensing (Song et al., 2014; Takashima et al., 2011; Tian et al., 2015), medical imaging (Morris et al., 2014), drug delivery, and catalysis (Ma et al., 2010; Song et al., 2010). Rieter et al. (2007) synthesized a Eu-doped and Tb-EDTA monoamide functionalized Gd(BDC)1·5(H2O)2@SiO2 (BDC=1,4-benzenedicarboxylate) derivative as a ratiometric luminescent sensor for DPA. A green luminescence was observed when ethanolic dispersions of the above MOF were mixed with DPA solutions prepared at varying concentrations; the green luminescence resulted from the formation of a Tb-EDTM-DPA complex. Such luminescent reactions were sensitive to DPA concentrations up to ∼48nM. A nanoscale metal organic framework, Eu2(FMA)2(OX)(H2O)4·4H2O (NMOF 1; FMA = fumarate, OX = oxalate), was also utilized to detect DPA. Its feasibility was tested with the CTAB surfactant-based Bravais-Friedel-Donnay-Harker (BFDH) method (Xu et al., 2012b). Adding DPA to the ethanolic NMOF1 solution led to a gradual increase in MOF luminescence intensity due to intramolecular energy transfer between DPA and Eu3+. This assay allowed DPA detection up to 2ppm. Despite the potential of the above quoted MOFs in DPA sensing, those methods rely on the use of nanocomposites and invariably required complicated preparation steps, use of organic solvents, and high-temperature synthesis conditions. Recently, a Tb3+/Eu3+ co-doped Bio-MOF was proposed as a ratiometric sensor for DPA (Zhang et al., 2016). The BIO-MOF was synthesized by mixing adenine, 4,4′-biphenyl dicarboxylic acid, and zinc acetate dihydrate in a DMF: water solution (27:2). The cation exchange with lanthanide ions (Tb3+/Eu3+) led to the synthesis of the Tb3+/Eu3+ co-doped Bio-MOF, which emitted an orange-red color due to the energy transfer from Tb3+ to Eu3+. With the addition of DPA, the emission turned green as the original energy transfer mechanism (i.e., Tb3+ to Eu3+) became dominated by energy transfer from DPA to Tb3+. This technique was highly sensitive (34nM) and specific to offer an effective visual detection of DPA, but only for concentrations >500nM DPA. Despite these benefits, the above-described sensor system has certain limitations, including its lengthy and complicated development procedure, need for non-aqueous solvents, and high preparation temperatures.

In this study, we report the first example of a Tb-MOF-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of DPA in an aqueous medium. Unlike previous reports, we used Tb-MOF, which can be synthesized from an aqueous medium to maximize the compatibility of the target analyte. Fluorescence detection was achieved via a direct sensor-analyte interaction without the need for pre-treatment steps. The water-soluble Tb-MOF ‘Tb(BTC)(H2O)6’ could be conveniently synthesized at room temperature using terbium nitrate as the metal source and trimesic acid as the organic ligand. The synthesized Tb-MOF was tested for photoluminescence quenching-based sensing of DPA across a wide concentration range.

All chemicals used in the study were of analytical grade and used without any further purifications. Terbium nitrate pentahydrate (99.99%), trimesic acid (H3BTC), and DPA (2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid; 99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (India). Isonicotinic acid and p-phenylene diacetic acid were purchased from Merck (India). Ultrapure water was used for all aqueous solution preparations.

The Tb-MOF was prepared at room temperature according to a previously published protocol (Yang et al., 2012). Briefly, equimolar (0.1mmol) solutions of Tb(NO3)3.5H2O and benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) were prepared individually in deionized water (10mL) and ethanol (10mL), respectively via stirring for 10min. The above two precursor solutions were then mixed with vigorous stirring to immediately form a white precipitate. The stirring was continued for about 1h, after which the accumulated white precipitate of the synthesized MOF product was collected through centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 15min. The separated Tb(BTC)(H2O)6 MOF crystals were washed 5 times with ethanol and then subsequently with water. The finally obtained product was then left to dry in air at room temperature (RT).

The three-dimensional supramolecular network structure of Tb-MOF consists of a Tb atom that is nine-fold coordinated by three oxygen atoms from the carboxlyate groups of the linker (trimesic acid) and six aqua ligands to form the structure presented in Fig. 1.

The absorption and emission spectra of the synthesized MOF were recorded using the Varian UV–vis–NIR (Cary 5000) and photoluminescence (Cary) spectrophotometers, respectively. The MOF morphology was investigated using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, 4300SE/N; Hitachi) and a powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) (Xpert Powder; Panalytical) system.

To measure the DPA response of the sensory system, 500µL of DPA prepared at different aqueous concentrations (5nM to 1000µM) was mixed with 500µL of Tb-MOF suspended in water. These contents were incubated for about 15min at room temperature (RT), followed by analysis of their fluorescence intensities at 545nm (excitation wavelength of 290nm). The effect of the presence of other aromatic molecules, namely isonicotinic acid and p-phenylene diacetic acid, was also investigated to determine the specificity of the Tb-MOF against interfering substances. Here, all of our reported readings are based on an average of triplicate (or more) readings.

The FE-SEM investigations verified the formation of rod-like structures in the Tb-MOF ( Fig. 2). Tb-MOF investigated in this study is among a few MOF structures that can be dispersed in an aqueous medium. As such, this property should have broad applicability for the detection of biomolecules, including DPA. The structure's crystallinity was verified through PXRD studies ( Fig. 3a). The PXRD pattern of synthesized MOF was structurally similar to that described in a previous study (Yang et al., 2012). The fluorescent nature of the Tb(BTC)(H2O)6 crystals was revealed by the confocal laser scanning image (Fig. 3b). The samples were excited by a laser light of 488nm and showed distinct fluorescence when visualized with a band-pass filter of 520–580nm. A grey-scale optical image (Fig. 3c) further corroborated the FE-SEM morphology data. The FTIR spectra of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6 is shown in Fig. 3d and exhibits the characteristic asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of carboxylate groups in typical frequency ranges of 1490–1590cm−1 and 1380cm−1, respectively (Cai et al., 2013). The absence of stretching vibrations from C=O (at 1730–1690cm−1) and C–OH (at 1280cm−1) bands of free organic linker (BTC) molecules highlight successful formation of the desired MOF complex, indicating an absence of leftover precursors (Guo et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and emission characteristics of Tb(BTC)(H2O)6
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum of synthesized Tb(BTC)(H2O)6 shows a characteristic broad peak at 230–300nm ( Fig. 4a), which is attributable to the charge-transfer process between the O2− and Tb3+ ions and to the π–π* electron transitions of the organic ligand (Chen et al., 2007; Liu et al., 2010; Xiao et al., 2009; Xu et al., 2012a). The emission spectrum of the MOF suspension (1mg/mL in DI water) is shown in Fig. 4b. The characteristic transitions of the Tb3+ ion can be observed at 488, 544, 581, and 620nm due to the 5D4-7F6, 5D4-7F5, 5D4-7F4, and 5D4-7F3 transitions, respectively (Cai et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies of photoluminescence detection of endospores
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesized Tb-MOF was utilized for the detection of DPA, which is an important marker of bacterial endospores. Different DPA concentrations were introduced into 500-µL (1mg/mL) suspensions of Tb-MOF solution, and the resulting changes in the luminescence intensities were measured (λexcitation=290nm). The dilution effect was corrected to establish final DPA concentration estimates. We recorded the sensor response at a photoluminescence (PL) peak of 545nm, at which signal intensity was best. The PL intensity of Tb-MOF gradually decreased with increasing DPA concentration ( Fig. 5a). The reduction in sample PL intensity was able to attain a stable PL pattern within 10min, confirming a reasonably fast response time for this sensing technique. This reduction can be linked to the decrease in energy transfer efficiency of the constituent metal ion (Tb3+) in response to DPA (Cable et al., 2007). Furthermore, the oxygen (of carboxylic acid groups) and nitrogen (of aromatic ring) atoms present on DPA can coordinate with the Tb3+ ions to substitute for the Tb-BTC coordination modes with a Tb-DPA complex (Tan et al., 2014). The combination of the above two factors can effectively lead to a PL quenching phenomena. Fig. 5b shows the logarithmically linear relationship between PL intensity and DPA concentration (1nM to 5µM). A statistically acceptable logarithmic linearity (regression coefficient =0.99) was obtained within the tested concentration range. The detection limit of our assay, when computed in terms of limit of detection (LOD), was calculated as 0.04nM (LOD =3σ/slope of the calibration curve, where σ is the standard deviation during blank measurements). This LOD is much lower than DPA doses known to be harmful to humans (≥0.1µM) (Bailey et al., 1965). A comparison of our sensor's performance compared to previously reported methods is presented in Table 1. Tb-MOF clearly offers an enhanced sensitivity level over a broad range of DPA analyses. As such, it confirms the improved suitability of the selected MOFs as efficient sensory materials for the identification and quantitation of spores in various samples.

Selectivity studies of Tb-MOF toward DPA molecules were conducted by testing the effects of other possible interfering organic molecules, e.g. isonicotinic acid (10µM) and p-phenylene diacetic acid (10µM). As shown in Fig. 6, the sensory material produced PL-quenching signals only in the presence of DPA (5µM). Therefore, highly selective DPA detection by Tb-MOF was likely achieved, because we observed no evident interference effects (e.g., the two tested compounds). The sensor could even produce a distinguishable response when DPA was mixed with other molecules.

Herein, a Tb-based metal organic framework, [Tb(BTC)(H2O)6], was synthesized by adopting a facile route for room-temperature aqueous synthesis. Unlike previous studies, Tb-MOF synthesized in aqueous medium offered a valuable new approach to conveniently and reliably quantify DPA, a potential marker of bacterial endospores. The mixing of the Tb-MOF fluorescence probe with DPA led to a fairly quick reduction in the photoluminescence signal. The PL-quenching signal maintained a highly linear relationship with increasing DPA concentrations in the range of 1nM to 5µM with a very low limit of detection (0.04nM). The developed assay thus allowed label-free optical detection of DPA in aqueous samples with high selectivity. Further scope of this work may include the demonstration of the sensor for the detection of bacterial spores in real biological and environmental samples. Such system can also be integrated as a solid-state optical sensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge that financial assistance was provided by CSIR-India (Grant no. OMEGA/PSC0202/2.2.5). The authors thank the Director of CSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh. KHK also acknowledges support from a grant of the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), which is funded by the Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (MEST) (No. 2006–0093848) and by the Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture, Science, & Technology Development (Project title: Study on model development to control odor from hogpens, Project No. PJ01052101), which is funded by the Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea.
